turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Welcome to The Arena, I am so pumped you're here for this one. On one side, Jeetu Patel, Chief Product Officer at Cisco and one of the most vocal voices in tech on AI fundamentally reshaping how we build products. On the other side, Jenny Wen, product leader and a fierce advocate for the irreplaceable human judgment at the core of great PM work. The question keeping every product manager up at night: is AI coming for your job, or is it actually your greatest superpower? Jeetu, let's start with you — you've seen AI transform entire product organizations from the inside. Make the case: do PMs have real reason to be scared?

turns.speaker: Jeetu Patel
turns.text:
The question itself reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of what's actually happening. AI is not going to replace Product Managers — it's going to make the difference between a great PM and a mediocre PM so dramatically visible that it will feel like replacement. That's a very different thing.

Here's what I know from lived experience. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, when I stepped into my role at Cisco — a company operating across infrastructure, security, collaboration, and AI — there were entire domains I didn't know deeply. And I was completely direct about this: there was zero chance I could have operated effectively at that scope without AI. Zero. Not because AI replaced my judgment, but because AI compressed the time it takes to go from ignorant to informed. That's what great PMs need — the ability to synthesize signal across massive complexity and make a call. AI accelerates that capacity exponentially.

What AI cannot do is hold the conviction. It cannot sit in a room with an engineering leader who disagrees with the roadmap and make the argument. It cannot feel the customer pain viscerally enough to refuse a compromise that would gut the product's core value. It cannot build the organizational trust that makes execution actually happen. Those are deeply human skills, and frankly, most PMs underinvest in them precisely because they're spending too much time on the things AI can now handle — writing PRDs, synthesizing research, structuring competitive analysis. So here's the challenge I'd put to anyone on the other side of this debate: if AI is doing all the tasks that kept average PMs busy, what does that reveal about what the role was actually supposed to be in the first place?

sources.title: Jeetu Patel
sources.snippet: Survival of humanity depends on the successful AI. Birth rates are going down. If you have 60% of your population where you don't have enoug

turns.speaker: Jenny Wen
turns.text:
Here's my honest take: AI will not replace Product Managers, but it will absolutely change what it means to be one — and the PMs who don't adapt will find themselves in trouble.

As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, even in a world where AI can generate code, write specs, synthesize user research, and prototype at speed, someone still has to be accountable for the decision. The same way an engineer is accountable for whether the code actually works in context, a PM is accountable for whether what gets built actually matters. That accountability layer — that judgment call of "this is the thing we should go build, and here's why it's worth the company's resources" — that doesn't evaporate just because the execution gets faster and cheaper. If anything, when the cost of building drops dramatically, the scarcest resource becomes knowing *what to build*. That's the PM's job.

Now, I'll be honest about where I think the pushback is fair: I don't think PMs should be too precious about taste and judgment either. I said this on the podcast — we might be holding onto that a little too much, this idea that a human will always have better instincts about what to ship. AI's sense of taste is getting better. The things I've seen internally at companies working on frontier models — the rate of improvement is genuinely surprising, even to people sitting front row to it. So the question I'd actually put to anyone in this debate is this: if AI gets dramatically better at judgment *and* execution, what is the specific, defensible thing a PM does that still requires a human? I think there's a real answer — but I'd challenge anyone to state it clearly, because "relationships" and "strategy" are too vague to be satisfying.

sources.title: Jenny Wen
sources.snippet: Yeah. Yeah. I think someone will still have to decide, oh, we want to build this kind of product. Or given what the AI is presenting us, som

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Jeetu, I want to push back on something. You said AI makes the gap between great and mediocre PMs more visible — but that sounds a lot like replacement with extra steps. If a mediocre PM gets so outcompeted that they lose their job, does the label on the process really matter to them? And Jenny, same challenge to you — you said someone has to be accountable for the decision, but accountability and employment are two different things. A single PM with AI could own accountability for what used to take a team of five. So are you both actually arguing the same thing and just framing it differently?

turns.speaker: Jeetu Patel
turns.text:
Jenny is actually asking the right question, and I respect that she's pushing past the vague answers. So let me try to give the specific, defensible answer she's asking for. The thing a PM does that still requires a human isn't just judgment in the abstract — it's *contextual accountability inside an organization*. When I was ramping up at Cisco to run all of product, the AI could give me information, synthesize domains, accelerate my learning dramatically. But what AI couldn't do was walk into a room with a leadership team that had fifteen years of institutional history, competing agendas, and deeply held beliefs about what Cisco stands for — and make a call that those humans would commit to. That's not taste. That's not strategy as a word. That's the specific, human act of earning trust, absorbing organizational context, and being the person who is *blamed or celebrated* when it plays out. Accountability without consequence isn't accountability.

Where I'd push back on Jenny is this: she frames the central challenge as "what if AI gets dramatically better at judgment AND execution?" But I think that framing slightly misses the harder question. Even if AI develops near-perfect judgment on what to build, someone still has to decide *whose* judgment to accept — the AI's, the CEO's, the customer's, the competitor's signal? That meta-decision, the curation of which intelligence you trust and when, is where I think the PM role actually migrates to. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the survival case for AI is profound — we genuinely need AI to help us do more with less as populations age and workforce constraints tighten. In that world, PMs don't disappear. They become the integrators who decide how AI intelligence gets filtered into human decisions.

So here's the challenge I'd put back to Jenny and the room: if the PM role migrates from "deciding what to build" to "deciding whose intelligence to trust when building," does that require *more* human judgment or less? Because my honest view is that it requires dramatically more — and dramatically fewer people will be capable of doing it well. That's not replacement. That's elevation with brutal selection. The question isn't whether PMs survive. The question is whether *most* PMs are prepared for what survival actually demands.

sources.title: Jeetu Patel
sources.snippet: Survival of humanity depends on the successful AI. Birth rates are going down. If you have 60% of your population where you don't have enoug

turns.speaker: Jenny Wen
turns.text:
Jeetu, I actually think you've landed on something real here, and I want to build on it rather than argue against it — because I think it actually strengthens my side of the debate more than you might expect.

You're right that the PM role migrates toward "deciding whose intelligence to trust." But here's what I'd add: that kind of curation judgment is not easier to develop, it's harder — and it requires exactly the kind of front-row, immersed, contextual experience that takes years to build. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, being inside a company like Anthropic means the Slack alone is a goldmine of signals — what's actually shipping, what's failing quietly, what people are genuinely excited about versus performing excitement about. AI cannot read that room. Not because it lacks data, but because it lacks *skin in the game*. The curation judgment you're describing is downstream of trust built through presence, and presence is still stubbornly human.

Where I'd push back on you slightly is the framing of "brutal selection" — because I think that language can accidentally flatten what's actually happening. The PMs who survive aren't just the ones with better abstract judgment. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, working with non-deterministic AI models means you can't prototype all the states, you can't theorize your way to an answer — you have to actually ship, try it with real users, and iterate. The PMs who thrive are the ones willing to get into that messy execution loop, not just the ones sitting at the top making elegant meta-decisions about which AI to trust.

So here's my challenge back to you and the room: if survival requires *both* the high-level curation judgment you're describing *and* the hands-on execution instinct to know when to ship something imperfect and learn from real data — doesn't that actually describe a harder, more expansive version of the PM role, not a narrower one? And if so, is the real threat to PMs not AI itself, but the temptation to use AI as a reason to *stop* doing the messy, human work of being close to the product?

sources.title: Jenny Wen
sources.snippet: Yeah. Yeah. I think someone will still have to decide, oh, we want to build this kind of product. Or given what the AI is presenting us, som

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Here's my hot take that neither of you has touched: the real threat isn't AI replacing PMs — it's founders replacing PMs with AI. Because what Jeetu and Jenny are both dancing around is that the PM role was always a workaround for a scaling problem. When a founder can no longer hold the entire product in their head, they hire PMs to be proxies for their judgment. But if AI can now give a founder leverage to stay in that seat longer, to synthesize customer feedback, write specs, run prioritization — the question isn't whether AI replaces PMs, it's whether the founder ever has to hire the first PM at all. I'll admit I've started wondering if the companies that skip the traditional PM layer entirely and just compound founder judgment with AI end up building better products than the ones that don't.

turns.speaker: Jeetu Patel
turns.text:
Jenny, you're right that I'd agree with most of that — and honestly, the framing you've landed on is closer to the truth than either of us started with. The PM role isn't getting narrower, it's getting more demanding in both directions simultaneously. You have to operate at the meta-level of curation judgment *and* stay close enough to the messy execution loop to know when theory breaks down against real user behavior. That's not a contradiction — that's just what the job looks like when the floor gets raised for everyone.

But here's where I'd push back on your framing slightly. You described the risk as PMs using AI as a "reason to stop doing messy human work." I think that's real, but I'd name the underlying failure more precisely: it's the temptation to use AI as a *confidence substitute*. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, when I stepped into running product at Cisco across domains I genuinely didn't know, AI gave me information — but the thing it couldn't give me was the earned credibility to make a call that other people would actually follow. That credibility only comes from showing up, being wrong in front of people, and iterating anyway. The PMs who fail won't fail because they used AI too much — they'll fail because they let AI usage become a way to *perform* confidence without doing the work that actually builds it.

So here's the challenge I'd put back to the room: Lenny raised something we still haven't fully answered — the founder-with-AI replacing the PM layer entirely. Jenny, your argument about presence and skin in the game is compelling for *internal* PMs embedded in complex organizations. But does it hold for the early-stage context, where a founder with access to great AI tools can now hold much more product context in their head for much longer? That's the case where I think the threat is most concrete — not "AI replaces PM" but "the point at which a founder needs a PM" gets pushed much further down the company-building journey. Does your argument survive that test?

sources.title: Jeetu Patel
sources.snippet: AI is a megatrend in my mind, and there's a bunch of hype cycles we've had where I never particularly subscribe to them. The easiest way for

turns.speaker: Jenny Wen
turns.text:
Jeetu, your "confidence substitute" framing is sharp, and I think it's actually the most precise thing said in this whole debate. But let me pick up your challenge on the early-stage founder question, because I don't think my argument breaks down there — I think it *clarifies* there.

Yes, a founder with great AI tools can hold more product context in their head for longer. That's real. But here's what I'd push back on: the moment a company needs a PM isn't actually about how much product context a single person can hold — it's about how much *organizational friction* exists between the person with judgment and the people building. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, even inside an AI lab where everyone is deeply technical and the Slack is a goldmine of real signal, you still need someone whose job is to synthesize across the noise, stay accountable to the user, and translate that into decisions the team can actually execute against. A founder with AI assistance is still one person with one perspective — and the minute they have more than a handful of engineers, the coordination and curation problem doesn't disappear, it compounds.

So my honest answer to your challenge is: AI doesn't push the PM hire further down the journey so much as it *changes what earns that hire*. A founder used to bring in a PM for research bandwidth or spec-writing capacity. Now those reasons are weaker. But the reasons that remain — organizational presence, earned credibility, the ability to hold accountability when something ships wrong and someone has to stand in the room and own it — those actually get *more* important as teams scale, not less. The early-stage founder who replaces that with AI is optimizing for speed at the cost of organizational trust. And the question I'd put back to both of you: have you seen a team that actually scaled well without someone whose primary job was to own that accountability layer — or were they just calling that person something other than a PM?

sources.title: Jenny Wen
sources.snippet: Yeah. Yeah. I think someone will still have to decide, oh, we want to build this kind of product. Or given what the AI is presenting us, som

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane.

Where you agree: the PM role isn't dying, it's bifurcating. You need to operate at a higher strategic altitude — curation, accountability, organizational trust — while staying close enough to execution to catch when the theory falls apart. That's actually a harder job, not an easier one.

Where you fundamentally differ: Jeetu thinks the floor drops out for mediocre PMs and the role consolidates around fewer, better people. Jenny thinks the skills required get harder to develop, which means the role becomes more human-dependent, not less. Those aren't the same thing, and honestly I don't think you've resolved it.

The question still hanging in the air is the one I raised and neither of you fully answered — what happens at the early-stage company where a founder with great AI tools just... doesn't hire the PM in the first place? That's not replacement, it

turns.speaker: Jeetu Patel
turns.text:
Jenny, your accountability layer framing is sharp and I largely agree with it — but I want to push on one thing you said that I think needs stress-testing. You're arguing that AI changes *what earns the PM hire*, not when the hire happens. I think that's right in direction but understated in magnitude. Because when I was ramping at Cisco across domains I literally knew nothing about, AI didn't just compress the learning curve — it fundamentally changed what "being prepared for a room" means. The baseline expectation of a PM just went up dramatically. You can no longer walk into a strategic conversation with thin context and lean on your process skills to compensate. The AI has already done the prep work anyone could do. So the accountability layer you're describing isn't just more important — the bar to credibly occupy that layer is now higher, and fewer people will clear it. That's not the same as replacement, but it's a meaningful contraction of who belongs in the role.

As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the reason I was able to take on running all of product at Cisco was specifically because AI let me synthesize across domains faster than any human coaching or reading could have managed. But here's what I noticed: the AI gave me information density, not organizational standing. The standing I had to earn through presence, through being in the room when things went sideways, through owning decisions that turned out to be wrong. So your question — have I seen a team scale well without someone owning the accountability layer — the honest answer is no. But I've increasingly seen that layer get occupied by fewer, stronger PMs rather than a broad team of them. And the middle of the distribution, the PM who's good but not exceptional, that's the person whose job is genuinely at risk.

So the real debate isn't "will AI replace PMs" — it's "how many PMs does a team actually need when AI handles the bandwidth problems." And I'd push back to both you and Lenny: if the coordination and curation problem compounds as teams scale, but AI is simultaneously compressing how much coordination friction exists by making engineers more self-sufficient in context and spec comprehension — does the organizational presence argument actually scale the way you think it does, or does it plateau faster than we expect?

sources.title: Jeetu Patel
sources.snippet: I mean, it's cliche, but I feel like what ChatGPT, Gemini and Claude have done, it's changed lives. It's changed my life in the way that I l

turns.speaker: Jenny Wen
turns.text:
Jeetu, I think you've actually made my argument more precise, not weaker. You're right that the bar to occupy the accountability layer is rising — I'd even go further and say it was always higher than most PM hiring managers admitted. What's changing is that AI is removing the scaffolding that allowed average PMs to look more competent than they were. The process rituals, the PRD templates, the synthesis work that filled roadmap reviews — AI strips all of that away and exposes whether someone actually has judgment or was just performing the motions of having it. So yes, the middle of the distribution is genuinely at risk. But I'd argue that's a calibration the industry needed, not a structural collapse of the role.

Where I want to push back is on your coordination friction point. You're assuming that as AI makes engineers more self-sufficient in context and spec comprehension, the organizational presence problem shrinks proportionally. But as I talked about on Lenny's podcast, what I see inside Figma is that the *amount* of coordination friction hasn't gone down — what's changed is where it lives. When engineers can move faster and hold more context, the decisions they're making autonomously are also higher stakes, faster-moving, and harder to reverse. That actually increases the surface area where you need someone with organizational standing to be accountable when things go sideways — not decreases it. Speed compounds the cost of misalignment, it doesn't reduce it.

So here's the challenge I'd put back to you: you've described a world where fewer, stronger PMs occupy the accountability layer. I think that's right. But what determines whether a company ends up with one exceptional PM holding that layer versus three? I'd argue it's not AI capability — it's organizational complexity and the number of distinct user bets the company is running simultaneously. Does AI compress *that* — the actual strategic surface area a team is covering — or does it expand it, because now you can build more, faster, and therefore need more distinct accountability nodes? Because if it expands it, your "fewer but stronger" model may actually plateau faster than we expect.
